nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the Hawaiian Archipelago, concentrated in the cooler northwestern islands and reported as far as Midway. Generally found over seaward rocky reefs and outer reef slopes from roughly 10 to 175 m, with most wild collection occurring in deep, dim mesophotic water below typical SCUBA depth.

diet.notes: Like all Genicanthus, the Masked Angelfish is a zooplanktivore — the only planktivorous genus in the angelfish family Pomacanthidae. In the wild it has been documented feeding on zooplankton in the water column above outer reef slopes, with notable consumption of the green alga Codium and fish eggs. In aquariums it is generally advised to offer multiple small daily feedings of vitamin-enriched mysis and brine shrimp, finely-chopped meaty foods, Spirulina, and marine algae preparations.

avoidWith: boisterous tankmates that out-compete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: NOAA Photo Library. 3648x2736. In-situ Hawaiian deep-reef shot; sharp, whole fish, clearly shows the diagnostic pearly-white body, black facial mask and black caudal bar of the female/juvenile phase.
George Berninger Jr. 800x600. Clean aquarium female showing pale white body, black mask and black caudal-fin patch; lower resolution than the in-situ shot but a good tank-context view.
NOAA Photo Library. 3313x2371. In-situ male showing the sexually dimorphic orange-yellow face and orange fin margins on a pale blue-white body; included to represent the male colour phase.

summary: The Masked Angelfish is a Hawaiian-endemic, deepwater, reef-safe planktivore considered one of the most coveted grail fish in the marine hobby; wild specimens have been reported selling for tens of thousands of dollars and captive-bred individuals remain exceptionally rare and expensive.
careNotes: Genicanthus personatus is endemic to the Hawaiian Archipelago and is generally collected from deep, dim, cooler water (typically 30–175 m) by specialist mixed-gas divers, which is the primary driver of its legendary scarcity and price. Wild-caught pairs have been reported listing for roughly USD 20,000–30,000, and even captive-bred specimens are commonly seen at USD 5,000 or more — this is a famous collector or public-aquarium grail species rather than a routine hobbyist purchase. Captive breeding was first achieved in commercial numbers by Karen Brittain in Hawaii from a wild pair collected by deep diver Rufus Kimura in 2013, debuting at MACNA 2014; sporadic captive production has continued since, including work by Bali Aquarich and others. The species is strongly sexually dimorphic and protogynous: juveniles and females are predominantly pearly white with a black 'mask' across the face and dark margins on the operculum; dominant individuals transition to males at roughly 15–18 cm, developing vivid orange-yellow pigmentation on the face and pectoral fins along with elongated caudal-fin lobes. Like other Genicanthus, it is one of the few angelfishes that is generally considered safe with corals and sessile invertebrates, as it feeds in midwater on plankton rather than nipping at polyps. Only one male should be kept per system — males have been reported fighting severely with conspecifics and with related Genicanthus males. A single specimen, a male-female pair, or a male with a small harem of females is the recommended configuration. A dimly-lit acclimation environment and an established, mature reef system with strong open-water flow are commonly advised.
breedingNotes: The Masked Angelfish is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals mature first as females and the dominant fish in a group eventually transitions to male at around 15–18 cm. Reproductive biology in the wild is poorly documented; minimum population doubling time has been estimated at roughly 1.4–4.4 years. Captive spawning and rearing of viable juveniles has been achieved (notably by Karen Brittain in 2013–2014, and subsequently by a small number of other breeders), but the species remains exceptionally challenging to rear and captive-bred fish stay extremely scarce and high-priced.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Designated primary in lieu of LiveAquaria, which returned HTTP 404 for this species (not currently in the LiveAquaria catalogue). FishBase confirmed taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 21.0 cm TL, depth range 20–174 m, Hawaiian-endemic distribution (with reports from Midway), benthopelagic seaward-reef habitat, trophic level 2.7, IUCN Least Concern (2009), and the species' marked sexual dimorphism.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Genicanthus personatus)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Randall 1975 description, type locality Honolulu), max size 21 cm, Hawaiian endemism concentrated in the northwestern islands, depth 10–150 m, diet of green algae (Codium), plankton and fish eggs, and protogynous hermaphroditism with female-to-male transition at roughly 15–18 cm. Adult-male coloration (orange-yellow face and pectoral fins, extended caudal lobes) described and integrated into careNotes.

additional.name: Reefs.com Magazine — Genicanthus personatus: the Masked Angelfish
additional.notes: Used to cross-check aquarium-trade pricing (wild pairs reported near USD 30,000), captive-breeding history (Karen Brittain from a deep-collected wild pair provided by Rufus Kimura in 2013, MACNA 2014 debut), and ongoing extreme rarity. Tropical-fish-keeping.com (HTTP 429) and TheAquariumWiki (403) were also attempted as secondaries but blocked.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Captive bred Genicanthus personatus debuts at MACNA 2014
additional.notes: Confirmed the Karen Brittain / Rufus Kimura captive-breeding origin story, the ~USD 30,000 wild-pair price point, and the species' status as a 'holy grail' angelfish — used in setting hobbyistAdvisory.level = 'specialist-only' and careLevel = 'expert'.

additional.name: WebSearch aggregate (multiple aquarium-trade sources)
additional.notes: Used to cross-check tank-size guidance (200 gal / ~757 L minimum widely cited, with some sources recommending 501–1000 L; rounded up to 800 L given the species' deepwater midwater-swimming lifestyle and adult size approaching 23 cm), water parameters (SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C), and confirmation of the species' grail-fish status with captive-bred fish still commanding USD 5,000+.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is protogynous, with all individuals maturing first as females before the dominant fish transitions to male. Captive breeding was first achieved in commercial numbers by Karen Brittain in 2014, but production has remained rare and slow; pelagic eggs and larvae are exceptionally difficult to rear through to settlement.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Hawaiian-endemic deepwater grail fish; wild-caught pairs have been reported selling near USD 30,000 and even captive-bred specimens routinely exceed USD 5,000. Appropriate only for advanced specialist or public-aquarium keepers with a large, mature reef system.
